issues.description: `lower_expr_iterative` is defined but never called anywhere in the workspace (confirmed via ripgrep — zero references). The recursive `lower_expr` at line 1686 remains the only lowering path. Beyond being dead code, this will trigger a `dead_code` warning; if the workspace treats warnings as errors (common in this repo), the build will fail.
issues.suggested_fix: Either delete the function entirely or wire it in at the call sites where stack overflow is actually a concern. If keeping it for later, remove it from this diff — speculative dead code is not acceptable, especially for a 'fix' that is not proven to work.

issues.description: The function does not actually produce the lowered expression for `root`. The stack-based loop pushes children onto `stack` after computing the parent's result via the still-recursive helpers, then appends all results to a flat `Vec<Option<Expr>>` with no parent-child composition. The final line `results.into_iter().last().flatten()` returns the LAST pushed result, which is a deeply-nested descendant (not the root). The root is the FIRST element in `results` because it is popped first. The function therefore returns the wrong expression for any non-leaf input, and for leaves it returns the leaf's result accidentally. This is silently wrong output, not a stack-overflow fix.
issues.suggested_fix: A real iterative lowering needs a two-phase approach: a post-order traversal that (a) pushes a 'visit' marker then children, and (b) when popping a marker, consumes the children's results from a value stack to assemble the parent node. Or, if simpler, keep the recursive lowering and raise the thread stack size via `stacker::maybe_grow` on entry. Do not merge the current implementation.

issues.description: The comment claims iterative lowering 'avoids stack overflow on deeply nested expressions.' This is false. The `binary_expression` arm calls `self.lower_binary_expr(node)` (which calls `lower_expr` on `left` and `right` recursively), `unary_expression` calls `lower_unary_expr` (recursive), `function_call` calls `lower_function_call` (recursive), and the `_` arm falls back to `self.lower_expr(node)` (fully recursive). Every non-leaf path still recurses, so the maximum recursion depth is identical to the baseline. Additionally, `binary_expression` both pushes its own children onto `stack` AND calls the fully-recursive `lower_binary_expr`, doubling the work for no benefit.
issues.suggested_fix: If stack-overflow protection is the real goal, use `stacker::maybe_grow` at the top of `lower_expr` (cheap, ~5 lines) or rewrite with a true post-order value stack. The current code does neither.

issues.description: The `parenthesized_expression` arm calls `continue` without pushing anything to `results`. Combined with the broken `.last()` return, if the root is a parenthesized expression the function may return whatever descendant result happened to land at the end of `results` — effectively returning the result of an unrelated subtree, or `None` if the paren-expr root has no lowered descendants.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove this function (see blocking findings above). Any correct rewrite must track a value stack and handle paren-exprs by forwarding the child result, not by silently dropping a slot.

issues.description: The match is missing many expression kinds that the real `lower_expr` handles: `range_expression`, `conditional_expression`, `match_expression`, `lambda_expression`, `quantifier_expression`, `quantity_literal`, `list_literal`, `set_literal`, `map_literal`, `ad_hoc_selector`, `index_access`, `member_access`, `qualified_access`, `instance_qualified_access`. All fall through to `_ => self.lower_expr(node)` (recursive), so even if the algorithm were correct, deeply-nested ranges / matches / lambdas would bypass it entirely.
issues.suggested_fix: If keeping this function, mirror every arm of `lower_expr`. Better: unify with `lower_expr` so there is one source of truth for kind dispatch.

issues.description: The `parenthesized_expression` child-filtering logic (`child.is_named() && child.kind() != "(" && child.kind() != ")"`) duplicates the identical logic already in `lower_expr` at line 1713. The `kind() != "(" && kind() != ")"` checks are also redundant because tree-sitter marks bracket tokens as unnamed — `is_named()` already excludes them. This is the same dead filter that exists in the recursive path; copying it twice is a missed opportunity to centralize.
issues.suggested_fix: Drop the `kind()` checks (kept for parity if you must), and extract a single `paren_inner_child(node)` helper used by both paths.

issues.description: No test is added. A claim that this fixes stack overflow on deeply nested expressions must be backed by a regression test that (a) constructs an input with nesting depth that previously overflowed the default 8 MB thread stack (typical threshold is 50k-200k deep), and (b) verifies the resulting `Expr` tree. Without such a test, we cannot verify the 'fix' works nor prevent future regressions.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a unit test that builds a synthetic deeply-nested binary expression (e.g., `1+1+1+...` repeated 100k times), lowers it, and asserts the resulting `Expr` depth equals the input. Run it inside `std::thread::Builder::new().stack_size(...)` to get a deterministic result.

issues.description: Even ignoring correctness, the function pre-allocates nothing for `stack` / `results`, and for `binary_expression` walks children with `walk()` AND then recomputes them inside `lower_binary_expr` — O(2·children) work per node. On pathological inputs this is strictly slower than the recursive path it is meant to replace.
issues.suggested_fix: Covered by the correctness rewrite — eliminate the duplicated traversal and pre-size with a modest capacity hint.

comprehensive_reviewer.summary: This diff adds a `lower_expr_iterative` function that is (1) entirely dead code — not called anywhere and will trip `dead_code` warnings, (2) algorithmically broken — it accumulates a flat `Vec` of results with no parent-child composition and then returns `.last()`, which is a deep descendant rather than the root, (3) doesn't achieve its stated purpose because every non-leaf arm still delegates to the fully-recursive `lower_binary_expr` / `lower_unary_expr` / `lower_function_call` / `lower_expr` helpers, and (4) silently drops the root slot for `parenthesized_expression` via `continue`. It also misses ~13 expression kinds that the real `lower_expr` handles, duplicates the paren-inner filtering logic, and ships without any test demonstrating the stack-overflow scenario it claims to fix. The function should not be merged — either delete it, or replace it with a real post-order value-stack lowering (or, much simpler, use `stacker::maybe_grow` to extend the thread stack on demand) and back it with a regression test that constructs a deeply-nested input.
